name: Anas ibn Malik
title: Anas ibn Malik (RA): The Servant of the Beloved
meta_description: The story of Anas ibn Malik (RA), the boy his mother gave to serve the Prophet Muhammad for ten years. He grew into the narrator of the Prophet's daily life, and saw him in a dream every night for the rest of his life.
keywords: Anas ibn Malik, story of Anas ibn Malik, servant of the Prophet, Khadim Rasul Allah, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories
lede: He was ten years old when his mother took him by the hand, walked him to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and said she had nothing else worthy to give. Only this son. Please take him, and let him serve you for as long as you wish.

This is the story of Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him): the boy who spent ten years at the door of the Prophet ﷺ, who never once heard a harsh word from him, and who would later teach the world what it was like to stand that close to such a man.

paras: When the Prophet ﷺ arrived in Madinah, the people of the city brought him gifts to honour him. Anas was a young orphan, his father gone, and his mother Umm Sulaim (may Allah be pleased with her) had little to offer. So she offered the most precious thing she had.

She came to the Prophet ﷺ and said there was not a man or woman among the Ansar who had not given him something, and she had nothing to give except this son of hers. Take him, she said, and let him serve you. Then she added a request: please, pray for him.

The Prophet ﷺ made a prayer that was as complete as a prayer can be. He asked Allah to increase Anas in wealth, to increase him in children, to give him a long life, and to forgive his sins. Anas would live to see every part of that prayer answered.

Umm Sulaim's home stood right beside the apartments of the Prophet ﷺ. Every morning Anas would step out of his own door and go straight to the door of the Messenger, ready to begin the day. For the next ten years, his life revolved entirely around that man.

He had no fortune to lose by giving these years away. People might wonder what a boy gives up by being handed to a life of service. Anas gave up nothing. He had been given the greatest opportunity of all: to wake each day to the face of the Prophet ﷺ, and to close each day having walked him home.

The Prophet ﷺ called him by tender names. Sometimes Unays, little Anas. Sometimes simply, my son. He once held the boy's two ears and called him the one with two ears, the kind of gentle teasing that meant only affection. Anas carried the name Khadim Rasul Allah, the servant of the Messenger of Allah, with quiet pride for the rest of his life.

sections.h2: Ten years, not one harsh word
paras: If anyone could have been treated carelessly, it was this boy. He was an orphan, a servant, young and easy to overlook. Instead, Anas gave one of the most striking testimonies in our history. He said he had never seen a man with more beautiful character than the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

He served him for ten years, he said, and in all that time the Prophet ﷺ never hit him, never cursed him, never said a single bad word to him. He never even frowned in his face. Never once gave him a look of displeasure.

There is a famous moment Anas loved to tell. The Prophet ﷺ sent him on an errand, and on the way the boy got distracted and went off to play with other children, forgetting the task entirely. After a while he looked up and saw the Prophet ﷺ had come and was sitting nearby, watching him, and laughing. He asked gently, little Anas, did you go where I asked you to go? Anas said, yes, I am on my way. That was the whole of the rebuke.

Because he was beside the Prophet ﷺ every single day, Anas became one of the great narrators of his life. It is said that the home life of the Prophet ﷺ comes to us mainly through our mother Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her), and his life among the people comes to us mainly through Anas, because he was the one who was with him outside the door, every day, for ten years.

He could describe the Prophet ﷺ down to the smallest detail. He counted seventeen grey hairs in all in his hair and beard. He saw how the Prophet ﷺ treated his own children with a mercy Anas had never seen in anyone. He was there, holding the moment, when the Prophet's infant son Ibrahim died in his arms, and the tears fell.

He watched the Prophet ﷺ walk into the homes of his companions and call for the children first, joke with them, place his hand on their heads, and pray for them. He saw him sit with the poor who came asking, and never turn them away empty. Over two thousand reports of this kind come from Anas, and there is not one of them that makes you think the Prophet ﷺ was anything but gentle. Every single one makes you love him more.

Anas did not only watch. He tried to live what he saw. The companions said that if you wanted to see the prayer of the Prophet ﷺ, you should look at the prayer of Anas. Abu Hurairah (may Allah be pleased with him) said he had never met anyone whose prayer resembled the Prophet's more closely. Anas prayed at night until his feet swelled, just as his teacher had done.

The Prophet ﷺ did not only give him an example to copy. He taught him the inner work behind it. He told him, blessed is the one who is busy with his own faults instead of the faults of other people. And he gave him an advice Anas never forgot: my son, if you can wake and sleep with no malice in your heart toward anyone, then do so, for that is part of my way. Whoever gives life to my way loves me, and whoever loves me will be with me in Paradise.
This was the heart of it. The Prophet ﷺ had once told him plainly how to follow this path, and the Qur'an said it in the same breath:

ayah.text: Say, ‘If you love God, follow me, and God will love you and forgive you your sins; God is most forgiving, most merciful.’

paras: Anas had thought about this. He had spent his whole young life beside this man, and he worried about the day they would be parted. He once asked the Prophet ﷺ to intercede for him on the Day of Judgement, and the Prophet ﷺ promised he would. Then, with the innocence of a boy who always knew where to find his teacher, Anas asked, where will I find you? Look for me at the bridge, he said. And if I do not find you there? Then at the scales. And if not there? Then at the fountain.

When the Prophet ﷺ lay dying, it was a Monday, and Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him) was leading the prayer. Suddenly the Prophet ﷺ drew back the curtain of his room, which looked out over the mosque, and stood with whatever strength he had left, gazing at the rows of his people praying. Then he smiled, and his face was so bright it looked like a page of the Qur'an. The companions almost lost their focus in prayer, turning to catch a glimpse of him. He motioned for them to complete their prayer, and let the curtain fall. It was the last time Anas saw him alive.

Anas was family enough to help bury him. He said the day the Prophet ﷺ entered Madinah, the whole city seemed to light up, and the day he died, it was as if the lights were switched off. As they laid him in the earth, the warmth seemed to leave their hearts. Fatimah (may Allah be pleased with her) turned to him in her grief and asked how he could bear to place soil over her father's face.

Anas was given a gift after that day that almost no one else has been given. The Prophet ﷺ had said that whoever sees him in a dream has truly seen him, for the devil cannot take his form. Anas lived more than eighty years after the Prophet ﷺ died, and he used to say that not a single night passed without seeing his beloved one in his sleep. When he told this, he would weep.

He spent those eighty years doing one thing above all. He sat in the mosque in Basra and told people about the Prophet ﷺ, until the greatest scholars of the next generation, Hasan al-Basri, Ibn Sirin, al-Zuhri, and others, gathered around him to listen. Many of them counted as part of that generation only because they had met Anas. He had become a living bridge back to the Messenger ﷺ.

His long life was not without pain. In his old age the tyrant al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf summoned him to humiliate him in public for the sides he had taken in the conflicts of the time. Anas, who had once been called little Anas in love, now heard the same name said in scorn. He answered only with the words, to Allah we belong and to Allah we return. Later he said he could have spoken a word that would have ended the man, but he held it back, because he did not want blood spilled in his name. He had learned from the Messenger ﷺ never to become the oppressor.

He died past the age of a hundred, the last of the major companions to leave this world. He liked to introduce himself as the last man among them who had prayed toward both directions of prayer. At his own request, he was buried with a few belongings of the Prophet ﷺ that he had kept all his life: a small staff, a strand of hair, a piece of cloth. He carried them into the grave.

lessons_intro: His story is not built on battles or great speeches. It is built on nearness, attention, and a heart that kept faith for a hundred years.

lessons.text: Anas gave ten years to serving someone else, and gained the richest life of all. The work we think is beneath us is often where the real blessing waits.
Ten years without a single harsh word turned a servant boy into a man who loved his teacher for life. People remember how we made them feel long after they forget what we asked of them.
Anas did not just admire the Prophet ﷺ. He copied his prayer, his patience, and his clean heart. Admiration that never becomes action changes nothing.

lessons.bold: A clear heart is a daily choice.
lessons.text: Sleep and wake with no malice toward anyone, the Prophet ﷺ told him. It is the quiet discipline of letting go of spite, renewed every single night.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ whose mother, Umm Sulaim, gave him to serve the Prophet when he was about ten years old. He served him for ten years in Madinah and became one of the most prolific narrators of his life and sayings.

faq.q: Why is Anas ibn Malik so important to Islamic history?
faq.a: Because he was with the Prophet ﷺ every day for ten years, he narrated over two thousand reports about the Prophet's character, worship, family, and daily conduct. Much of what we know about how the Prophet ﷺ lived among people comes through Anas.

faq.q: How long did Anas ibn Malik live?
faq.a: He lived past the age of a hundred and was the last of the major companions to die. He spent more than eighty years after the Prophet's death teaching the next generation about him in Basra.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Anas ibn Malik?
faq.a: That service is an honour rather than a loss, that gentleness leaves a lasting mark, and that loving the Prophet ﷺ means trying to live as he lived, with a clean and patient heart.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be tested on a battlefield or asked to give a great speech. We are tested in the small, daily nearness of ordinary life: how we speak to the people who serve us, how we treat the child who forgets the errand, what we carry in our hearts when we lie down at night. Anas spent ten years close enough to see all of this in the Prophet ﷺ, and what he saw was patience that never cracked.

His life is an invitation to pay attention. To soften the word we were about to say. To empty the heart of spite before sleep, and to begin again in the morning. That is the way he loved, and it is a way still open to anyone who wants it today.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Anas ibn Malik (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 3:31). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

He was ten years old.

His father was gone, and his mother had nothing else worthy to give.

So she gave him.

"Take my son, and let him serve you."

The Prophet ﷺ prayed for the boy.

Increase him in wealth. Increase him in children. Give him long life. Forgive his sins.

Anas would live to see every part of it answered.

For **ten years** he stood at the door of the Prophet ﷺ every morning.

Later he said: in all that time, he never hit me, never cursed me, never even frowned in my face.

One day the Prophet ﷺ sent him on an errand.

The boy got distracted and went off to play.

The Prophet ﷺ found him, laughing, and asked gently: "Did you go where I asked you to go?"

That was the whole rebuke.

Because he was always there, Anas saw everything.

Over **two thousand** reports of how the Prophet ﷺ lived among people come from him.

Not one of them makes you love the Prophet ﷺ less.

He did not just watch. He copied.

The companions said: if you want to see the prayer of the Prophet ﷺ, look at the prayer of Anas.

He prayed at night until his feet swelled.

On his last Monday, the Prophet ﷺ drew back the curtain and smiled at his praying people.

His face was as bright as a page of the Qur'an.

It was the last time Anas saw him alive.

For **eighty years** after, Anas said he saw the Prophet ﷺ in a dream every single night.

He spent those years telling the world about him.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
When the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ came to Madinah, a widow named Umm Sulaim brought him her young son. She had nothing else worthy to give. "Take him," she said, "and let him serve you."

Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) was about ten. He would spend the next ten years at the Prophet's door, and he later said that in all that time he never once heard a harsh word from him. Never even saw him frown.

He grew into one of the great narrators of the Prophet's daily life, and for more than eighty years after the Prophet ﷺ passed, he said he saw him in a dream every single night.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The boy his mother gave to the Prophet
email.alt_subject: Ten years, and not one harsh word
email.preview: His mother had nothing else worthy to give. So she gave him her son.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

When the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ arrived in Madinah, the people brought him gifts. A widow named Umm Sulaim had little to offer, so she offered the most precious thing she had.

She brought her young son, Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him), and said there was no one among the Ansar who had not given the Prophet something, and she had nothing to give except this boy. Take him, she said, and let him serve you. Then she asked the Prophet ﷺ to pray for him.

Anas was about ten years old. For the next ten years, he stepped out of his door each morning and went straight to the door of the Prophet ﷺ to begin the day.

Later in his life, people asked him what those years were like. His answer is one of the most moving testimonies we have. He served the Prophet ﷺ for ten years, he said, and in all that time the Prophet never hit him, never cursed him, never said a single bad word to him. He never even frowned in his face.

There was a day the Prophet ﷺ sent him on an errand, and the boy got distracted and went off to play with other children, forgetting the task entirely. When he looked up, the Prophet ﷺ was sitting nearby, watching him, laughing. He asked gently, little Anas, did you go where I asked you to go? That was the whole of the rebuke.

Because he was beside the Prophet ﷺ every single day, Anas became one of the great narrators of his life. Over two thousand reports of how the Prophet ﷺ lived among people come through him, and not one of them makes you love the Prophet less.

The Prophet ﷺ once gave him an advice he never forgot: if you can wake and sleep with no malice in your heart toward anyone, then do so, for that is part of my way.

There is a quiet lesson in his life that is easy to miss. Gentleness leaves the deepest mark. Ten years without a harsh word turned a servant boy into a man who loved his teacher for the rest of his life, and saw him in his dreams every night for over eighty years.

May Allah be pleased with him, and join us in the company of those who loved the Prophet ﷺ.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was ten years old when his mother took him by the hand, walked him to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and said she had nothing else worthy to give. Only this son. Please take him, and let him serve you for as long as you wish.

Anas ibn Malik (may Allah be pleased with him) lived right beside the Prophet's home, and every morning he stepped out of his own door and went straight to the door of the Messenger ﷺ, ready to begin the day. For ten years, his whole life revolved around that one man.

Years later he gave a testimony that is hard to forget. He served the Prophet ﷺ for ten years, he said, and in all that time he was never hit, never cursed, never spoken to harshly. The Prophet ﷺ never even frowned in his face. Once he was sent on an errand, got distracted, and went off to play. The Prophet ﷺ found him and only laughed, and asked gently, little Anas, did you go where I asked you to go?

Because he was always there, Anas saw everything, and he became one of the great narrators of the Prophet's life among the people. He copied what he saw too. The companions said that if you wanted to see the prayer of the Prophet ﷺ, you only had to look at the prayer of Anas.

On the Prophet's last Monday, as the people prayed, the curtain of his room drew back. The Prophet ﷺ stood with whatever strength he had left and smiled at them, his face as bright as a page of the Qur'an. Then he let the curtain fall. It was the last time Anas saw him alive.
Anas helped to bury him, and said that the day the Prophet ﷺ came to Madinah the whole city lit up, and the day he died it was as if the lights were switched off.

He lived more than eighty years after that, the last of the great companions to leave this world. He spent those years telling people about the Prophet ﷺ, and he used to say that not a single night passed without seeing his beloved one in a dream. When he said it, he would weep.
storybook.pullquote: I served him for ten years, and he never once frowned in my face.
storybook.reflection: Anas was not a warrior or a chief. He was a boy who was simply given the chance to stay close, and pay attention. What changed him was not a single great moment but ten years of unbroken gentleness. His life asks us to soften the word we were about to say, and to empty our hearts of spite before sleep. That is how he loved, and it is a way still open to us.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
